title: Linear Attention Alternatives
concept: Architectures like State Space Models (SSMs) or Mamba scale attention linearly ($O(N)$), enabling near-infinite context windows theoretically.

exercises.prompt: What happens to standard Transformers when you feed them extremely long books? try:

options.text: They get faster the more text you give them.
They get exponentially slower and run out of memory.
They automatically summarize the text into bullet points.

exercises.explain: Standard Transformer models compare every word to every other word, causing the work to grow quadratically (O(N²)) as texts get longer.

cards.title: The Memory Wall
cards.body: Have you ever tried to read a massive book, only to forget the first chapter by the time you reach the end? Standard AIs struggle with this too—the longer the text, the slower they get. But a new breed of AI architectures can read almost infinite text without breaking a sweat! Let's find out how.

cards.title: What is Linear Attention?
cards.body: Linear Attention alternatives, like State Space Models (SSMs) and Mamba, are new AI designs that process data in a straight line. Instead of comparing every word to every other word (which is slow), they update a small, smart memory room step-by-step. This keeps the work amount low and predictable, even if the text is millions of words long!

cards.title: The Summary Notepad
cards.body: Think of standard Transformer attention like a giant group chat where every new message forces you to re-read every single past message from the very beginning. As the chat grows, it takes forever! Linear attention (like SSMs) is like having a smart assistant who updates a summary notepad after each message. You only need to look at the notepad, not re-read the entire history!

cards.title: Reading a Whole Library
cards.body: Imagine you want an AI to read an entire 800-page fantasy book series and write a sequel. A standard Transformer might run out of computer memory halfway through the first book. An SSM model like Mamba can ingest the whole series smoothly, maintaining a constant memory footprint, and instantly write the next chapter.

cards.title: The Active Memory Myth
cards.body: Many people think that to remember long stories, an AI must keep every single word in its active memory forever. This is a myth! SSMs prove that by dynamically updating a smart, compact summary, an AI can remember the plot perfectly without keeping millions of raw words active at once.

cards.title: Quadratic vs. Linear Scaling
cards.body: Compare how Transformers and SSMs scale up as you feed them longer text. Notice how the work explodes for one, but stays in a straight line for the other!

left.text: Doubling the text makes the workload jump by 4 times. It quickly crashes with long books.

right.text: Doubling the text only doubles the work. It can handle massive books easily.

exercises.prompt: How do State Space Models (SSMs) avoid the slow math of standard attention?

options.text: They compare every word with every other word.
They only look at the very last word and ignore all history.
They update a fixed-size internal state as they read.
They make the text shorter by deleting vowels.

exercises.explain: Standard attention compares everything with everything, which makes it quadratic. SSMs process step-by-step with a fixed state, keeping the cost linear.

exercises.prompt: State Space Models (SSMs) scale linearly, which makes it possible to process much longer texts than traditional Transformers.
exercises.explain: Linear scaling (O(N)) means doubling the text length only doubles the work, allowing for theoretically infinite context windows.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how modern SSM architectures scale with longer texts.
exercises.sentence: By updating a compact memory state, architectures like Mamba scale [blank] with sequence length.
exercises.explain: Because SSMs and Mamba scale linearly, they can handle massive amounts of text without requiring exponentially more computer power.

exercises.prompt: Identify the system that is using a State Space Model (SSM) approach:

options.text: System A: The AI that runs out of memory after 2,000 words.
System B: The AI that reads 100,000 words while using a constant, small amount of memory.
System C: The AI that only remembers the last 5 words you typed.

exercises.explain: System A is a standard Transformer because its memory cost grows quadratically (squared). System B is an SSM (like Mamba) because its memory stays constant or scales linearly. System C is just a simple hard limit.

exercises.prompt: How do State Space Models (SSMs) manage to process extremely long sequences of text without slowing down to a crawl?

options.text: They require quadratic compute power to look at older words in the text.
They compress past information into a fixed-size running summary.
They delete all previous text completely to save computer memory.
They run slower than standard Transformers on short sentences.

exercises.explain: Standard Transformers suffer from quadratic cost because they compare every word to every other word. State Space Models (SSMs) like Mamba process sequence elements step-by-step while maintaining a constant-size internal state (like a running summary). This lets them scale linearly, making them incredibly fast even with massive amounts of text.
